title: What China’s military learnt from Trump’s Venezuela raid

transcript.text: I think the term decafitation strike is
a bit unsettling I [laughter] think to
the average person. Um but as we as we
can see the importance of this is
clearly increasing. So it seems likely
that Beijing is going to look closely at
the US strike on Venezuela. What lessons
do you think the leadership in Beijing
will be hoping to draw? I don't think
they'll be looking at this as a sort of
template for an attack on Taiwan, but I
do think they will look very closely at
different components of it to help hone
their own abilities in particular to
conduct a decapitation strike on
Taiwan's leadership because, you know,
we know that's something they've talked
openly about for some time as one part
of, you know, a potential assault on
Taiwan and it seems to be something that
they are focusing on increasingly over
the last few years probably as a result
of Russia's failure to take out uh
Ukraine's president Vladimir Zalinski in
the early stages of uh the Russian
invasion and then his subsequent success
in rallying both domestic and
international support for for resistance
against that invasion. For someone who
looks at the military far less closely
than you two, I think the term
decafitation strike is a bit unsettling.
I [laughter] think to the average
person. Um, but as we as we can see, the
importance of this is clearly
increasing. How is the Chinese military
preparing for these decapitation
strikes?
>> So, one of the ways is through physical
simulations. In 2015, China's state
television uh showed footage of a a
similar mockup of the Taiwan
presidential palace uh somewhere in the
desert in northern China uh and they
were using that to practice an assault
on Taiwan's leadership. And that
training site has actually tripled in
size uh since 2020.
>> How well prepared is China to pull off a
decapitation operation of the kind it's
been practicing for? Presumably they've
made some progress through all those
simulations and other drills they've
been doing in recent years, but I think
there are still a number of key
obstacles and probably the main one is
that the people's liberation army has
yet to achieve the kind of high level
integration of space, air, land, sea,
cyber capabilities um that are essential
to pull off the kind of complex
operation that we saw in in Karacus.
There's been a big effort by Xiin Ping
um on over the last 10 years to try and
make those exercises uh more focused on
sort of real combat. But um I think
that's still, you know, a real challenge
for the PLA.
[music]
